children.text: The shift towards more fuel efficient cars had two principle causes. First, the US experienced spikes in oil prices that resulted from foreign policy crises (for example, in 1973, Arab states put an embargo on oil shipments to the US in retaliation for American support for Israel in the Yom Kippur War). The skyrocketing price of gas meant US consumers were looking for cars that got more miles per gallon than the traditional Detroit-made “gas guzzler.” This is related to the second cause: import competition from Japan. Japanese automobile companies (such as Honda, Toyota, and Nissan) were producing affordable, smaller, more fuel efficient cars that were attractive to US consumers. As a result, US automakers lost considerable business. In 1965, the “Big Three” Detroit automakers (Ford, GM, and Chrysler) sold almost 90% of cars sold in the US; by 1980, that figure was less than 74%.
